title: Applying danish oil to bare wood furniture
subtitle: Build a durable penetrating oil finish on bare or stripped timber with successive rubbed coats
excerpt: How to prepare bare or stripped wood furniture for danish oil, apply successive thin coats by rubbing in with a cloth and cutting back between coats, recognise when the wood is saturated, and apply a final buffed coat for a low-sheen penetrating finish.
sourceNotes: Furniture Restoration and Repair, William Cook. The Woodfinishing Book, Michael Dresdner. Rustins product guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A blend of drying oils (typically linseed and tung oil) with a resin component and solvent carrier, formulated to penetrate the timber surface and cure in place rather than forming a film on top. The result is a low-sheen protective finish that feeds the wood and resists moisture while leaving the natural grain visible and the surface touchable rather than glassy. Successive coats build depth and durability.

The lifting of surface wood fibres caused by the solvent or water content in the first coat of any finish. After the first coat of danish oil cures, the surface will feel slightly rough. Cutting back with 400 grit or 000-grade steel wool removes the raised fibres and restores a smooth surface before the second coat.

Lightly abrading a dried finish coat with fine abrasive — 400 grit paper, 000-grade steel wool, or a fine sanding pad — to remove dust nibs, raised grain, and minor surface irregularities between coats. Cutting back does not remove the coat; it levels the surface so the next coat lies flat. The dust produced is wiped away before the next coat is applied.

content.text: Danish oil is applied as a penetrating finish: it feeds the timber rather than forming a film on the surface. The technique is forgiving: thin, rubbed coats are the method for both the first application and all subsequent coats. A piece finished with three or four coats of
will develop durability and a low sheen that is maintained by re-oiling as the wood dries over time.

Sand the bare timber to 180 grit with the sanding block, working with the grain at all stages. Use the card scraper to level any machine marks or raised areas before sanding: scrapers cut faster than abrasive on flat grain. Sand to 240 grit for the final preparation pass. Wipe all dust away with a clean dry cloth. The surface must be grease-free: any wax or oil contamination will prevent the

onto a lint-free cotton cloth: never a foam applicator, which traps air. Rub the oil into the surface with the grain, applying a thin even coat across the whole piece. Allow the oil to penetrate for five to ten minutes, then wipe off any surplus oil still sitting on the surface. Surplus oil that dries on the surface rather than in the grain creates a sticky patch that takes days to cure. Leave to dry for a minimum of six hours.
Once the first coat is fully dry, the surface will feel rough due to
lightly with 400 grit paper on the sanding block, working with the grain. The aim is to smooth the surface, not to remove the oil coating. Wipe away all dust with a clean cloth before the next coat.
Apply the second and third coats of
in the same way as the first: thin coat, allow to penetrate, wipe off surplus, dry fully, then

. Three coats is the minimum for a piece in regular use. The wood is saturated when a coat no longer absorbs into the surface within five minutes and sits visibly as a film: this is the point to stop adding coats and simply wipe the surface clean.
For the final coat, apply a very thin film of

and buff it into the surface with a clean dry cloth until the surface is dry to the touch. Allow to cure for 24 hours before the piece is returned to use. Full hardness develops over several days as the oil crosslinks in the timber. Oily cloths used with danish oil are a fire risk: lay them flat to dry outdoors or submerge in water before disposal.

On dense, close-grained timbers such as teak and iroko, thin the first coat of danish oil 10% with white spirit. This helps it penetrate rather than sit on the surface. For a harder finish, apply one coat of hard wax oil over the cured danish oil. This adds surface protection without building up a film like lacquer. Danish oil is not suitable under shellac or nitrocellulose lacquer: the oil prevents adhesion of solvent-borne film finishes.

attrs.symptom: Sticky patches remain on the surface several days after application
attrs.cause: Surplus oil left on the surface rather than wiped off; too much oil applied in one coat.
attrs.fix: Wipe the sticky area with a cloth dampened with white spirit to remove the uncured surplus. Allow to dry fully. Re-apply a thin coat, wiping off all surplus within five minutes.

attrs.symptom: Blotchy dark patches appear on the finished surface
attrs.cause: Oil absorbed unevenly into end grain or soft porous areas; surface not prepared evenly.
attrs.fix: Before oiling, apply a dilute coat of shellac sanding sealer to the end grain and porous patches to reduce their absorption. Allow to dry and sand lightly before oiling — this equalises the take-up across the surface.

attrs.symptom: Surface feels rough after the final coat despite cutting back between coats
attrs.cause: Grain raising on the final coat not sanded; dust contamination before the last coat dried.
attrs.fix: Cut back the final coat lightly with 400 grit paper, wipe clean, and apply one more very thin finishing coat buffed dry with a clean cloth.
